content:
{
  "title": "Cross-Lingual Demonstration Mining for Prompting Low-Resource Languages",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) underperform on multilingual or low-resource language tasks due to limited training data and ineffective prompt design. Improving in-context learning for such languages remains a major challenge.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed globally, robust performance across diverse languages is essential for fairness and accessibility. Traditional prompting approaches rely on high-resource language examples or manual curation, limiting scalability. There is an urgent need for prompting strategies that systematically leverage multilingual resources to support low-resource tasks.",
  "method": "We propose Cross-Lingual Demonstration Mining (CLDM), a prompting framework that automatically retrieves semantically similar in-context examples from high-resource languages, translates them into the low-resource target language, and uses them as demonstrations in the prompt. The system ranks candidate examples using multilingual sentence embeddings to ensure context relevance, and translation quality is enhanced via round-trip consistency checks. The prompt is constructed with a balanced mix of translated and native low-resource examples, dynamically selected per input instance to maximize relevance.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CLDM on the XTREME benchmark, focusing on text classification and NER tasks in languages with very limited training data. Baselines include the best-performing standard in-context prompting (random and manual selection) and retrieval-augmented prompts without translation. Metrics are F1-score and accuracy, and we ablate each component to isolate the effect of cross-lingual example mining versus translation quality."
}
